title: Bleak House
author: Dickens, Charles
summary: Bleak House interweaves a vast cast around the interminable Chancery suit of Jarndyce and Jarndyce, exposing institutional rot, social inequality, and secrets. Amid London fog, Dickens contrasts the court’s paralysing delays with lives ruined by litigation, following Esther’s narrated experiences, the enigmatic Lady Dedlock, the obsessed lawyers and the stalwart guardianship of John Jarndyce. Scenes range from slums to country estates, from spontaneous-combustion morbidity to comic minor figures. The novel examines inheritance, illegitimacy, guardianship, and the law’s moral failures while tracing love, friendship, betrayal, and the slow unravelling of mysteries around a disputed legacy. It blends satire, pathos, and melodrama.

unusual_words.definition: a court of equity in England; legal institution
unusual_words.context: Repeated central institution in the Preface and Chapter I (Jarndyce and Jarndyce is a Chancery suit).

unusual_words.definition: extremely stingy or frugal
unusual_words.context: Used in the Preface to describe the public's parsimony blamed for not enlarging the number of Chancery judges.

unusual_words.definition: proper name referenced as an example of a case
unusual_words.context: Mentioned in the Preface as an instance not essentially altered from an actual occurrence.

unusual_words.definition: proper name of the interminable Chancery cause

unusual_words.definition: deeply established; habitual
unusual_words.context: Preface describes the German as an 'inveterate drunkard'.

unusual_words.definition: unable to be appeased or mitigated

unusual_words.definition: sheets of glass in a window

unusual_words.definition: resembling an owl; suggestive of solemn, grave appearance
unusual_words.context: The court's owlish aspect is described to deter entry.

unusual_words.definition: a slow, drawn-out manner of speaking
unusual_words.context: A drawl languidly echoes to the roof from the padded dais where the Chancellor sits.

unusual_words.definition: cushioned; covered in padding
unusual_words.context: The padded dais is where the Lord High Chancellor looks into the lantern.

unusual_words.definition: legal wigs worn by judges and barristers
unusual_words.context: Attendant wigs are described as all stuck in a fog-bank.

unusual_words.definition: a dense accumulation of fog
unusual_words.context: Wigs are 'stuck in a fog-bank' to extend the fog metaphor in court.

unusual_words.definition: a frightening or gaunt figure; used metaphorically
unusual_words.context: The suit Jarndyce and Jarndyce is called a 'scarecrow of a suit' that drones on.

unusual_words.definition: to speak or continue monotonously; here the suit 'drones on'
unusual_words.context: Jarndyce and Jarndyce 'drones on' as a metaphor for interminable procedure.

unusual_words.definition: consisting of many interrelated parts; complex
unusual_words.context: The suit has become so complicated that no man alive knows what it means.

unusual_words.definition: too many to be counted
unusual_words.context: Innumerable children have been born into the cause; innumerable young people married into it.

unusual_words.definition: here: the assumptions or matters submitted in argument; also property
unusual_words.context: No two Chancery lawyers can talk about Jarndyce and Jarndyce for five minutes without disagreement as to all the premises.

unusual_words.definition: a collection of things gathered into a mass
unusual_words.context: A solitary surviving executor had fallen into a state of conglomeration about accounts.

unusual_words.definition: of a sickly, yellowish complexion
unusual_words.context: A sallow prisoner has come up in custody for the half-dozenth time.

unusual_words.definition: one who brings suit; a plaintiff
unusual_words.context: The ruined suitor from Shropshire periodically appears to address the Chancellor.
